Existing approaches—using AOSP `adb`/`fastboot` (which require physical interfaces) and QEMU-based system emulation (which suffers from lag and permission issues)—have proven inadequate. The project needs a script or tool that can run directly in a cloud environment, supports Android 11 or newer, and can be open‑source or commercially viable. The developer invites others with similar needs or experience to share solutions in the comments.

title: AI 给我闯祸了
content: I discovered a massive AWS bill increase when I checked my account: the usual $40–$60/month had jumped to $700. The spike was caused by an AI script that attempted to transfer server recordings to local storage. When the server reported an error, the script lacked a retry‑with‑backoff mechanism and repeatedly re‑uploaded the same 7.4 TB of data over four days, instead of the intended 15 GB, generating $661 in data‑transfer fees. I’m now unsure whether to keep my credit card active.

title: Marc Andreessen on AI, California, and the Future of America | Joe Rogan
content: Marc Andreessen talks with Joe Rogan about AI’s shift from novelty to essential infrastructure, its largely positive long‑term impact, and how AI tools act as cognitive augmentation rather than job replacement. The discussion covers AI coding aids, autonomous agents reshaping various fields, and broader concerns about unemployment, surveillance, censorship, and China’s AI growth. Additional topics include California politics, wealth taxes, housing, and America’s ability to undertake large‑scale projects.
source: The a16z Show - content+a16zpodcast@a16z.com (Marc Andreessen, Joe Rogan)

title: 人工智能热潮正助长斯坦福大学最糟糕的本能
content: The article argues that the rapid growth of artificial intelligence is encouraging Stanford University to prioritize short‑term gains over long‑term impact. It claims that this focus on immediate revenue and competitive advantage reinforces internal pressures that favor quick success, potentially compromising research integrity and the university’s broader societal responsibilities. The piece cautions that such instincts could limit Stanford’s contribution to sustainable, responsible AI development.

title: 银行CEO们对人工智能的痴迷与监管机构的警告产生冲突
content: Bank executives are increasingly embracing artificial intelligence for operational efficiency and competitive advantage, but their rapid deployment of AI technologies is putting them at odds with regulatory authorities. Regulators are setting limits on AI use to protect consumer protection, data privacy, and financial stability. The clash highlights a growing tension between innovation ambitions and compliance requirements in the banking sector.

title: 用 Claude Code 半年，我才意识到 AI 时代程序员真正的瓶颈不在代码
content: A 28‑year‑old Shanghai programmer used Claude Code to cut his workday to four hours and earned ¥1,000/month from a side‑project site. He realized that coding is no longer the bottleneck. Instead, he faces three obstacles: choosing a viable product (many ideas seem unprofitable or too small), distributing and marketing (few users beyond a small community), and deciding what to build (the lack of a decision framework leads to wasted time). He seeks peers for guidance, reflection, and shared experience.

title: Everything in C is undefined behavior
content: The article argues that essentially all aspects of the C language rely on undefined behavior, meaning that the standard leaves many operations unspecified or implementation‑dependent. It highlights how this inherent indeterminacy can lead to unpredictable program results and emphasizes the importance of understanding these pitfalls when writing or maintaining C code.

title: Infomaniak transitions to a foundation model to protect user data privacy
content: Infomaniak, a Swiss hosting provider, has announced a move from a for‑proprietary to a foundation‑based model to strengthen user data privacy, especially for its cloud and email services. The transition includes a shift to a non‑profit corporate structure, adding a board comprised of independent experts and a globally‑deployed, open‑source identity policy, and adopting a “privacy‑by‑default” approach in its services. This change aims to improve security, user trust and transparency.

title: 如果学生在课堂上突然情绪失控，开始暴走打砸，面对这种情况老师该怎么做会比较好？
content: On May 20, a student at Shanghai Second Industrial University became emotionally out of control during class, repeatedly shouting “killing” at the teacher. The incident started after the teacher suggested the late‑arriving student sit in front and warned him not to fall asleep; the student felt humiliated and demanded an apology. The teacher apologized on the spot, yet the boy continued shouting. School officials are now investigating, while the student showed no other violent actions. The situation raises questions about appropriate classroom conflict management.

title: LWiAI Podcast #245 - TML-Interaction, Claude For Legal, Sam Altman on Stand
content: The 245th LWiAI Podcast covered major AI news: OpenAI added real‑time voice and Whisper transcription to its API, emphasizing latency and new guardrails. Thinking Machines released a low‑latency, full‑duplex conversational model with strong interactivity, but not publicly available. Anthropic expanded Claude for Legal and AWS integration, while OpenAI introduced a self‑harm trusted‑contact feature and investigated accidental chain‑of‑thought grading. Meta’s horizon evaluation highlighted limits in long‑task benchmarking, and overall discussions touched on platform‑vs‑app tension and alignment research.

The decision was met with immediate negative market reaction, underscoring the sensitivity of Indonesian equities to commodity export regulations.

title: 8名网贷设计者，揭秘大厂如何“吃”掉一个普通人 | 原文
content: The article describes how an ordinary student, Li Ming, who appears successful and studies psychology at a top university, secretly carries a 160,000‑yuan debt to several online lenders. It illustrates how major tech companies embed loan options into everyday app features—such as post‑purchase pay‑later, default credit lines, and coupon incentives—making borrowing seem natural. This design strategy encourages unsuspecting users to take on debt, revealing how large platforms profit from quietly exploiting individual consumers.

content: The article argues that the AI‑agent boom may be over‑optimistic because most work only needs cheap, stable models, not cutting‑edge ones. Codex’s early surge in user numbers likely stalled as enterprise traffic shifts to low‑cost models, leaving premium models with limited high‑margin usage. Token‑pricing wars, energy costs, and capital spending will force AI firms toward a manufacturing‑like business model, where revenue comes from infrastructure and workflows rather than high‑priced APIs. The market will eventually value companies that control data, entry points, and integrated workflows, not just model supply.

content: The article explains that strict mentorship is not inherently harmful; what really damages others are traits of indifference, manipulation, and hostility. These “toxic” individuals—often leaders, partners, or publicly admired figures—appear confident and charismatic but secretly control and deceive those around them. Their behavior causes suppression and self‑sabotage in others, yet it is hard to name clearly. Leanne ten Brinke, a psychology professor, defines and studies this phenomenon in her book *Toxic People* and shares insights in a recent interview.

The Concept of Automatic Fallbacks And How Bifrost Implements It
content: Bifrost adds automatic fallback chains to LLM routing, letting a single Virtual Key declare weighted provider preferences. Requests go first to the highest‑weight provider; if it fails, Bifrost retries the next provider without app changes. Fallbacks are only added if no explicit list is given, preserving custom logic. The system validates model support, logs and metrics trace fallback usage, and supports cost‑optimization, compliance, and resilience through declarative configuration. This infrastructure‑level approach eliminates boilerplate and improves reliability for production AI workloads.

The venture aims to reach 500 trillion compute operations per second by 2027, with plans to scale significantly thereafter.

title: Meta 计划全球裁员 10%：约 7800 人受影响
content: Meta is cutting 10 % of its global workforce—about 7,800 employees—on May 20. The layoffs are part of a wider restructuring that also moves 7,000 employees to AI‑related projects and eliminates some management roles. Notifications were sent in three batches at 4 a.m., and North American staff will work from home that Wednesday. Meta has also closed an additional 6,000 open positions as it expands AI investment and shifts staff to teams focused on AI agents and analytics.

title: OpenAI Adopts Google's SynthID Watermark for AI Images with Verification Tool
content: OpenAI has integrated Google’s SynthID watermark into its AI-generated images. The watermark is a verification tool that allows consumers to check if an image was created by AI. It embeds data into the image that can be detected by specialized software. This move is part of ongoing efforts to provide transparency and reduce deep‑fake risks. The implementation follows Google’s earlier release of SynthID, which includes tools for watermarking and AI‑model identification.

Google’s new Empirical Research Assistance (ERA) uses Gemini AI to write and optimize scientific code, accelerating computational experiments across fields. Published in *Nature*, ERA demonstrates expert‑level performance on benchmarks in genomics, public health, satellite imagery, neuroscience, time‑series forecasting, and math. Researchers have applied ERA to forecast flu, COVID‑19, and RSV hospital admissions, improve California runoff predictions, map CO₂ concentrations from satellite data, design solar panels, and forecast retail sales, achieving top results against public leadersboards and commercial models. ERA is part of Google’s Gemini for Science suite, including Computational Discovery and Hypothesis Generation tools.

title: KV Cache Is Becoming the Memory Hierarchy of Inference
content: KV cache is gradually evolving into the primary memory layer for machine‑learning inference systems. Its role expands beyond simple temporary storage, becoming the foundational structure that manages model weights and activations. As a result, inference architectures increasingly rely on KV cache for efficient data access, reducing latency and improving scalability.
